tests.title: Testora Studio Listening Practice Test 01
tests.description: Original IELTS-style practice test created by Testora.

metadata.authorship: Original IELTS-style practice test created by Testora.
metadata.intro_script: This is a Testora Studio Listening Practice Test. The test will begin in five seconds.
metadata.intro_notice: The recording opens with a test identification, followed by exactly five seconds of silence and a neutral cue.

independent_simulation.focus: timed cold run and distractor audit

sections.title: Section 1 - Repair workshop booking
sections.instructions: Questions 1-10. Complete the booking form. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.
sections.passage:
Maya: Good afternoon, Harbour Repair Workshop. Maya speaking. How can I help?
Naomi: Hello. I would like to book a place on the beginners' bicycle maintenance session.
Maya: Certainly. Can I take your surname? Patel, that's P A T E L.
Naomi: Yes, that's right. I saw two dates online, but I was not sure which still had spaces.
Maya: The page briefly showed the seventeenth of July, but that session is now reserved for a school group.
Maya: The public beginners' session is on the eighteenth of July, and there are three places left.
Naomi: Perfect. I finish work at five thirty, so I hope it is not too early.
Maya: You will be fine. The workshop starts at six thirty and the doors remain open until six twenty-five.
Naomi: Do I need to bring tools? I have a small pump, but nothing for removing a wheel.
Maya: We provide all the tools, and an apron is included as well.
Maya: You only need to bring gloves. Ordinary gardening ones are suitable.
Naomi: Good. What does the session cost?
Maya: It is forty-two pounds, including replacement cables to practise with. There is no separate deposit.
Naomi: I was planning to drive. Is there parking behind the workshop?
Maya: There are only two spaces and both are needed for staff vans. The train station is a long walk.
Naomi: In that case I will take the bus. Route twelve stops across the road, doesn't it?
Maya: It does. The main doors will be locked by then because the shop closes at six.
Maya: Use the side entrance, beside the blue loading bay. We will put a workshop sign there.
Naomi: Could you text me if anything changes?
Maya: Of course. I have zero seven nine two zero, six zero seven nine, from your online enquiry.
Naomi: The last four digits should be six zero seven four, not six zero seven nine.
Maya: Thanks, I have corrected that. We also invite people to bring one damaged object for the recycling demonstration.
Maya: A steel or aluminium part is useful, but please bring aluminium if you have a choice because it is easier to cut safely.
Naomi: I have an old aluminium bottle cage. That should work.
Maya: Yes. I will email the booking confirmation today. If you need to cancel, reply to that email at least twenty-four hours beforehand.

questions.explanation: Aluminium is preferred because it is easier to cut safely.

questions.explanation: Cancellation is made by replying to the confirmation email.

sections.title: Section 2 - Coast discovery centre
sections.instructions: Questions 11-15. Label the map. Choose the correct letter A-H. Questions 16-20. Choose the correct answer A, B, or C.
sections.passage:
Lucas: Welcome to Northhaven Coast Discovery Centre. Before you explore, I will describe the site using the map on your screen.
Lucas: You are standing at the entrance at the bottom of the map. The main path runs straight towards the shore.
Lucas: Reception is on the right as you enter, but you do not need to go there if you already have a ticket.
Lucas: The ticket kiosk is immediately on your left, at point B, before the path reaches the first junction.
Lucas: Continue to the wooden footbridge. Just after crossing it, a narrow path turns right towards the reed beds.
Lucas: Follow that narrow path to point D, where you will find the bird hide.
Lucas: Back on the main path, the education garden is beside the pond.
Lucas: The picnic shelter is opposite the garden at point F, not beside the pond as an older leaflet suggests.
Lucas: For the kayak launch, keep going until the main path bends left around the laboratory.
Lucas: Take the short path on the seaward side of that bend. The kayak point is H, close to the beach.
Lucas: The toilets are not in the laboratory. Walk past the laboratory and the outdoor showers.
Lucas: They are at point G, at the end of the paved path, just before the dunes.
Lucas: Now, a few suggestions for planning your visit.
Lucas: Families often head directly to the beach, while the short film is popular when people are tired.
Lucas: For younger children, however, begin at the touch pool. A guide is always there to help them handle the animals gently.
Lucas: The seal talk normally starts at two o'clock on weekdays.
Lucas: Today the rescue team is returning late from a survey, so the talk cannot begin at its usual time.
Lucas: It has been moved to two thirty. The feeding demonstration will follow at three.
Lucas: You may photograph wildlife and use binoculars throughout the site.
Lucas: Please do not feed the birds, even with food bought in our cafe, because it changes their natural behaviour.
Lucas: Our outdoor programme changes with the weather.
Lucas: Light rain does not stop the shore walk, and in heavy rain we use the covered observation deck.
Lucas: Strong wind is different: the shore path closes, and the activity moves into the indoor laboratory.
Lucas: Entry to the centre is free. You may see a suggested donation at reception.
Lucas: That donation pays for fuel and maintenance for the small rescue boat, rather than the exhibits inside the centre.

sections.title: Section 3 - Student food-waste study
sections.instructions: Questions 21-25. Match each statement with the correct speaker. Questions 26-27. Choose the correct answer. Questions 28-30. Complete the notes. Write ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER.
sections.passage:
Dr Okafor: Let's review your proposed study of food waste in the campus dining hall. What have you decided so far?
Mina: We first planned to compare all three dining halls, but that would give us too much data for a six-week project.
Lucas: I still think two sites would make the results stronger.
Mina: I would rather study one hall properly. A shallow comparison would not tell the catering team what to change.
Dr Okafor: A single site is acceptable if you explain the limitation.
Lucas: My concern is that students may behave differently when they notice us measuring what they leave.
Mina: We could put the scales behind the tray-return screen, so nobody sees the measurements.
Lucas: That solves part of it, and I agree that we should avoid questioning people while they eat.
Lucas: But I still want short interviews afterwards, because the weights alone cannot explain why food was left.
Mina: I offered to conduct the interviews because I wrote the draft questions.
Lucas: You also know the weighing equipment. I have used interview software before and can code the responses faster.
Mina: Fair point. You take the interviews, then, and I will manage the measurements instead.
Dr Okafor: Good. Make sure your categories separate food that was unpopular from portions that were simply too large.
Mina: Portion size matters because the same serving is given to everyone.
Dr Okafor: Exactly. That is the strongest reason to record meal type as well as total weight; soup and bread cannot be compared as if they were identical.
Lucas: Should our title be 'How much food do students waste'?
Dr Okafor: That only describes quantity, while your interviews investigate motives.
Dr Okafor: Frame the main focus as the causes of edible food waste. The total weight becomes supporting evidence.
Mina: For the method, we could ask diners to estimate how much they left.
Lucas: Self-reported estimates are unreliable. Photographs might be better, although classifying them would take time.
Dr Okafor: Use direct observation of returned plates, supported by weighing. Save interviews for explaining the patterns.
Mina: We tested the observation sheet with twelve diners yesterday.
Dr Okafor: For the main study, collect data from sixty diners across lunch and dinner. Twelve is only enough for the pilot.
Lucas: I can clean the spreadsheet and send it to you on Wednesday.
Dr Okafor: Wednesday was the date in your old plan, before the dining hall changed its menu cycle.
Dr Okafor: Send the final data on Thursday instead, after you have captured the vegetarian menu.
Mina: And our final assessment is a written report, correct?
Dr Okafor: The report is for the catering manager. For this module, your assessed output is a poster, which you will present in week eight.

transcript_segments.speaker: Dr Okafor

sections.title: Section 4 - Introduction to soundscape ecology
sections.instructions: Questions 31-35. Complete the sentences. Write ONE WORD ONLY. Questions 36-40. Choose the correct answer A, B, or C.
sections.passage:
Lecturer: Today we will look at soundscape ecology, a field that treats environmental sound as data rather than background.
Lecturer: A soundscape contains biological sounds, physical sounds such as wind and water, and sounds created by people.
Lecturer: The key idea is not merely to list those sounds, but to study the relationships between living things and their environment through sound.
Lecturer: Animal calls perform several ecological functions.
Lecturer: They can attract mates, warn of predators, coordinate a group, or defend territories.
Lecturer: A recording therefore tells us more than whether a place seems pleasant to a human listener.
Lecturer: When analysing a recording, loudness describes the strength of a sound.
Lecturer: Frequency, by contrast, helps distinguish a low engine hum from a high insect call.
Lecturer: Researchers often display both time and frequency as a coloured image.
Lecturer: This image is called a spectrogram. It can reveal repeated calls that are difficult to notice by ear.
Lecturer: One short recording can confirm that a species called at a particular moment, but long-term monitoring is more powerful.
Lecturer: If recorders sample the same locations over months or years, researchers can compare seasonal patterns.
Lecturer: Repeated detections may also provide an indicator of abundance, although they are not a direct count of individual animals.
Lecturer: Human noise is one reason those comparisons require care.
Lecturer: A road does not necessarily cause birds to leave the entire area.
Lecturer: Traffic may instead mask their calls, making communication and detection more difficult even when the birds remain present.
Lecturer: A good monitoring design reduces avoidable differences between samples.
Lecturer: Recording whenever a researcher happens to be free would mix dawn, midday, and evening soundscapes.
Lecturer: Recording at the same time each day makes samples more comparable and separates daily cycles from longer changes.
Lecturer: Interpretation must also remain cautious.
Lecturer: Suppose a frog appears in Monday's recording but not Tuesday's.
Lecturer: We should say it was not recorded on Tuesday, not that it was absent. Wind or another sound may have hidden its call.
Lecturer: Consider a small city park monitored beside a road.
Lecturer: The weekday recordings contain steady traffic from the morning commute.
Lecturer: At the same hour on weekends, traffic falls and more short bird calls can be detected. That pattern may reflect reduced masking, not an instant rise in bird numbers.
Lecturer: Finally, sound recordings are valuable because they can be stored and checked again by different analysts.
Lecturer: They can reveal ecological change across time, but microphones do not explain every cause.
Lecturer: The strongest conclusion is therefore that acoustic evidence is a repeatable measure which must be interpreted alongside habitat, weather, and human activity.

transcript_segments.speaker: Lecturer
transcript_segments.text: The strongest conclusion is therefore that acoustic evidence is a repeatable measure which must be interpreted alongside habitat, weather, and human activity.

questions.text: Soundscape ecology studies ____ between living things and their environment.
questions.explanation: The field studies relationships through sound.

evidence.quote: study the relationships between living things and their environment

questions.text: Some animal calls are used to defend ____.
questions.explanation: Defending territories is one function of animal calls.

questions.text: ____ helps distinguish a low engine sound from a high insect call.
questions.explanation: Frequency differentiates low and high sounds.

questions.text: A visual display of time and frequency is a ____.
questions.explanation: The visual display is called a spectrogram.

questions.text: Repeated detections can indicate a species' ____.
questions.explanation: Repeated long-term detections may indicate abundance.

questions.text: How can traffic noise affect birds that remain in an area?
questions.explanation: Noise can mask calls and hinder communication or detection.

evidence.quote: Traffic may instead mask their calls

questions.text: Why should recordings be made at the same time each day?
questions.explanation: A consistent time reduces variation caused by daily cycles.

questions.text: What should a researcher conclude when a species is not heard?
options: It was not recorded in that sample
correct_answer: It was not recorded in that sample
questions.explanation: Non-detection does not prove absence.

questions.text: Why were more bird calls detected at weekends in the park example?
options: There may have been less traffic masking
correct_answer: There may have been less traffic masking
questions.explanation: Reduced traffic masking may reveal calls that weekday noise covers.

questions.text: What is the lecturer's main conclusion about acoustic evidence?
options: It explains the causes of every change
It is repeatable but needs contextual interpretation
It is useful only with direct animal counts
correct_answer: It is repeatable but needs contextual interpretation
questions.explanation: Recordings are repeatable evidence but need habitat, weather, and activity context.

evidence.quote: repeatable measure which must be interpreted alongside
